elements.sent-2411-001: Motivated by recent Event Horizon Telescope findings, we investigate constrained optics of rotating black holes in Einstein-Maxwell-scalar gravity theory.
elements.sent-2411-002: Precisely, we mainly study the parameter effects on two relevant optical concepts being the shadow and deflection angle.
elements.sent-2411-003: Using the Hamilton-Jacobi algorithm, we find certain shadow geometries being corroborated by observational data once appropriate constraints on a stringy coupling parameter $\beta$ are imposed.
elements.sent-2411-004: For such constrained regions of the black hole moduli space, we compute the deflection angle of light rays near to such black holes.
elements.sent-2411-005: Concretely, we show that this optical quantity can be split into two contributions describing the absence and the presence of the coupling parameter $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Then, we discuss and analyse such an optical quantity in terms of $\beta$ contributions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Keywords: Black holes, Einstein-Maxwell-Scalar theory, Shadow behaviors, Light deflection angle, EHT observational data.
elements.sent-2411-008: Recently, the study of the optical aspect of black holes becomes a warm subject being largely investigated using different approaches including analytical and numerical results brought either by Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) collaborations or by the gravitational wave detections \cite{A56,B10,A57,A58,A59,A60,LIGs,BGRA .}
elements.sent-2411-009: A close inspection reveals that two relevant concepts have been studied being the shadow and the deflection angle of the light rays near to several black holes \cite{Jaf,Yanxx,A154A,A65,A66,A68,A69,A74,A78,A81,MHER,HERDE,Novo,SV1 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: A particular emphasis has been put on such physical proprieties of black holes with non-trivial backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-011: In this way, the shadow aspect, providing predictions that can be analyzed using the falsification mechanism, has been largely studied via various techniques.
elements.sent-2411-012: Exploiting the Hamilton-Jacobi formalism, certain geometrical configurations of black hole shadows have been obtained and analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-013: More precisely, regular and irregular black holes, in many gravity theories, have been approached.
elements.sent-2411-014: Concretely, it has been shown that the shadows of the non-rotating black holes exhibit circle configurations with different sizes controlled by the black hole parameters including the charge.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, such perfect geometries have been deformed by introducing the rotating parameter providing non-trivial one dimensional curves known as D or cardioid ones\cite{A79,A159,SV2,A77 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Motivated by high-energy physical models such as string theory, the shadows of black holes in the Starobinsky-Bel-Robinson (SBR) gravity by introducing a stringy parameter have been investigated \cite{sbrh .}
elements.sent-2411-017: It has been revealed that this parameter can modify previous shadow results.
elements.sent-2411-018: Alternatively, other investigations relying on different stringy gravities have been also conducted.
elements.sent-2411-019: Among others, it has been provided various shadow geometries, aligning with EHT collaboration data through imposed constraints on the involved black hole parameters \cite{Lekbi,YASSS,Tsu,BBAM,AMM .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Moreover, many efforts have been devoted to study light behaviors near to certain anti de Sitter (AdS) black holes.
elements.sent-2411-021: This concerns the computation and the analysis of the deflection angle of light rays near to various black holes in different models, including those related to high-energy physics \cite{A95,A89,A96 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: For the AdS black holes, this optical quantity for both regular and non-regular black holes, with the presence or the absence of dark energy sectors, has been approached via graphical discussions by varying the relevant involved parameters.
elements.sent-2411-023: In particular, the deflection angle of light rays near to four and seven-dimensional AdS black holes, derived from M-theory compactifications, has been computed by unveiling the impact of the M-brane number coupled to the rotating parameter \cite{HRC,HADS,HMTD .}
elements.sent-2411-024: More recently, a special interest has been put on the study of black holes in modified Einstein gravity by implementing a possible coupling between scalar fields and gauge fields.
elements.sent-2411-025: In string theory, for instance, these scalar fields can be obtained from either the stringy spectrum including the dilaton and the axion or the compactification scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-026: Thermodynamic and optical behaviors of such black holes have been largely investigated furnishing interesting results \cite{Hirschmann, Yu, Al, Charmousis,Lu,hemd .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The aim of this work is to provide constrained optics of rotating black holes in Einstein-Maxwell-scalar (EMS) gravity theory.
elements.sent-2411-028: Precisely, we investigate certain parameter effects on two relevant optical concepts being the shadow and the deflection angle.
elements.sent-2411-029: Using the Hamilton- Jacobi algorithm, we find certain shadow geometries being corroborated by observational data by imposing appropriate constraints on a stringy coupling parameter $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-030: For such constrained regions of the black hole moduli space, we study the deflection angle of light rays near to such black holes.
elements.sent-2411-031: Precisely, we reveal that this optical quantity can be split into two contributions describing the absence and the presence of the coupling parameter $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-032: This work is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-033: In section II, we provide a concise discussion on prorating black holes in EMS theory in the presence of a stringy coupling parameter $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-034: In section III, we investigate the associated shadows.
elements.sent-2411-035: In section IV, we examine the deflection angle in terms of the stringy coupling parameter.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this section, we expose the black hole models in EMS gravity theory.
elements.sent-2411-038: This theory has been supported by superstring model scenarios where various scalar fields appear in natural ways, belonging to lower energy limits of supergravity in different dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-039: These scalars including the dilaton and the axion could couple to other spectrum gauge fields to generate extended gravity models going beyond the ordinary ones in four dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-040: Omitting the scenarios of the scalar fields originated from string theory compactification mechanisms, the scalar fields could play a relevant role in the construction of certain black hole models.
elements.sent-2411-041: Roughly speaking, we consider a model described by the following action \begin{equation}S = \int d^{4}x \sqrt{-g}(R - V(\varphi) - (\nabla\varphi)^{2} - K(\varphi)F^{2})\end{equation} where R denotes the Ricci scalar curvature.
elements.sent-2411-042: A close examination shows that the coupling function and the scalar potential could provide certain classes of black hole solutions.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the present work, we consider a solution associated with the following functions \begin{eqnarray}V(\varphi) = \frac{\lambda}{3} (e^{2\varphi} + 4 + e^{-2\varphi}) K(\varphi) = \frac{2e^{2\varphi}}{-2\gamma + \beta + \beta e^{2\varphi}}\end{eqnarray} where $\lambda$ can identified with the cosmological constant.
elements.sent-2411-044: It has been shown that an exact black hole solution in EMS theory could be obtained by taking $\gamma = -1$ \cite{Yu .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Moreover, the EMD theory can be recovered by taking $\beta=0$ \cite{Yu .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Considering the above function choice, one can obtain the rotating cosmological black hole metric in EMS gravity theory by applying the Newman-Janis algorithm without complexification \cite{Ainou .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this way, the resulting metric can be expressed as follows \begin{eqnarray}ds^2 = -\left( 1 - \frac{\sigma(r)}{H(r)}\right) dt^2 -\frac{2a^2\sigma(r)}{H(r)} sin^2 \theta dt d\phi + \left( h(r) + a^2 +\frac{a^2\sigma(r)sin^2 \theta }{H(r)} \right) sin^2 \theta d\phi^2\notag + \frac{H(r)}{\triangle(r)} dr^2 + H(r) d\theta^2\end{eqnarray} where $a$ is the rotating spin parameter.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}ds^2 = -\left( 1 - \frac{\sigma(r)}{H(r)}\right) dt^2 -\frac{2a^2\sigma(r)}{H(r)} sin^2 \theta dt d\phi + \left( h(r) + a^2 +\frac{a^2\sigma(r)sin^2 \theta }{H(r)} \right) sin^2 \theta d\phi^2\notag + \frac{H(r)}{\triangle(r)} dr^2 + H(r) d\theta^2\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-048: The metric functions are given by \begin{eqnarray}H(r) = h(r) + a^2 cos^2 \theta \triangle(r) = f(r)h(r) + a^2 \sigma(r) = h(r)(1-f(r))\end{eqnarray} where one has used \begin{equation}f(r) = 1 - \frac{2M}{r} + \frac{\beta Q^2}{h(r)} - \frac{1}{3}\lambda h(r), \qquad h(r) = r \left(1 -\frac{Q^2}{M r}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}H(r) = h(r) + a^2 cos^2 \theta \triangle(r) = f(r)h(r) + a^2 \sigma(r) = h(r)(1-f(r))\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-049: In this equation, $M$ and $Q$ are the mass and the charge of the black hole, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-050: In what follows, we would like to investigate the extended moduli space effect on the optical behaviors of this class of black holes.
elements.sent-2411-051: Precisely, we approach the $\beta$ contribution effects on the associated optical behaviors.
elements.section-2411-003: Constrained shadows of rotating EMS black holes
elements.sent-2411-052: Optical aspects of black holes have been largely investigated using analytical and numerical approaches.
elements.sent-2411-053: These black hole activities have been supported and encouraged by the observational results brought either by EHT collaborations or by the gravitational wave detections \cite{EventHorizon .}
elements.sent-2411-054: A close inspection shows that two relevant concepts have been studied being the shadow and the deflection angle of the light rays near to black holes \cite{Chakhchi,Gogoi .}
elements.sent-2411-055: In this section, we reconsider the study of such optical quantities for AdS black holes in EMS in terms of $\beta$ contributions.
elements.sent-2411-056: Precisely, we provide constraints on the studied black holes from the observational EHT data.
elements.sent-2411-057: These constraints will be exploited in the next section where a discussion on the deflection angle will be provided.
elements.sent-2411-058: In this part, we examine the shadows of rotating AdS black holes in EMS theory.
elements.sent-2411-059: It is recalled that the shadow of a black hole is defined as the apparent boundary or the critical curve which appears when light rays asymptotically approach an unstable circular orbit called photon sphere and return towards the observer.
elements.sent-2411-060: This information is encoded on the null geodesics around black holes.
elements.sent-2411-061: To approach the shadow optical aspects of the rotating black holes, we should establish certain relations using the Hamilton-Jacobi factorization based on the Carter method \cite{A81 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Concretely, for such black hole solutions, the four equations of motion are given by \begin{equation}H \dot{t} = \frac{h+a^2}{\Delta}\left[ E\left( h+a^2\right) -a L\right] +a\left[ L-aE\sin^2\theta \right] (H \dot{r})^2 =R(r) ( H\dot{ \theta})^2 =\Theta(\theta) H \dot{\phi} = \left[ L \csc ^2 \theta-aE\right] +\frac{a}{\Delta} \left[ E\left(h+a^2\right) -aL\right],\end{equation} where $E$ and $L$ are the energy and the angular momentum of the light rays, respectively.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}H \dot{t} = \frac{h+a^2}{\Delta}\left[ E\left( h+a^2\right) -a L\right] +a\left[ L-aE\sin^2\theta \right] (H \dot{r})^2 =R(r) ( H\dot{ \theta})^2 =\Theta(\theta) H \dot{\phi} = \left[ L \csc ^2 \theta-aE\right] +\frac{a}{\Delta} \left[ E\left(h+a^2\right) -aL\right],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-063: Solving the unstable circular orbit equations, the two needed impact parameters are obtained as follows \begin{equation}\eta=\frac{C}{E^2} =\frac{4 \Delta (r) h'(r) \left(a^2 h'(r)+h(r) \Delta '(r)\right)-4 \Delta (r)^2 h'(r)^2-h(r)^2 \Delta '(r)^2}{a^2 \Delta '(r)^2}\vert_{r=r_0}, \xi=\frac{L}{E}=\frac{a^2 \Delta '(r)-2 \Delta (r) h'(r)+h(r) \Delta '(r)}{a \Delta '(r)}\vert_{r=r_0}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: For the rotating AdS EMS black holes, the apparent shapes of the shadows are depicted in Fig.(\ref{rsa ) by considering the following celestial coordinates \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} X =\lim _{r_{ob } \rightarrow+\infty}\left(-r_{ob }^2 \sin \theta_{ob } \frac{d \phi}{d r}\right) Y =\lim _{r_{ob } \rightarrow+\infty}\left(r_{ob }^2 \frac{d \theta}{d r}\right), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $r_{ob }$ is the distance of the observer from the black hole.}
elements.sent-2411-065: It is denoted that $\theta_{ob }$ indicates the angle of inclination between the line of the observer and the axis of rotation of the black hole.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \begin{tabbing} \=\kill \includegraphics[scale=0.55]{P2.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.55]{P5.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.55]{P7.pdf} \end{tabbing} \caption{ \it Shadow behaviors of rotating AdS EMS black holes in terms of the charge, the cosmological constant and the stringy coupling parameter $\beta$.} \label{rsa} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-066: This figure provides the shadow configurations for certain black hole parameters.
elements.sent-2411-067: It is worth noting that the deformation of the black hole shadow from the circular apparent is linked to the no vanishing value of the rotating parameter.
elements.sent-2411-068: However, this effect is impacted by the contribution of the $\beta$ parameter.
elements.sent-2411-069: As shown in the first panel of Fig.(\ref{rsa ), increasing the $\beta$ value from zero to one induces a gradual transition from a cardioid-like configuration to a D-shaped form, with a reduced deformation and a smaller overall size.}
elements.sent-2411-070: For the behavior of the shadow against the charge variation, it is clear from the second panel that the charge contributes as a reducing parameter of the size matching with the previous study of the charged black holes.
elements.sent-2411-071: The point in this case is that the coefficient of the size deduction is more important for large value of the $\beta$ parameter.
elements.sent-2411-072: Interestingly, moving to the third panel of the Fig.(\ref{rsa ), the behavior of the cosmological constant is amazingly inverted.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Taking large negative values gives small shadow configurations.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Contact with empirical data via shadows
elements.sent-2411-074: To approach an alignment between rational predictions and empirical data, the following subsection presents an analysis of the shadow cast by rotating cosmological EMS black holes, incorporating relevant data from the EHT.
elements.sent-2411-075: Specifically, we exploit the EHT observational results for M87$^*$ and SgrA$^*$ to establish constraints on the parameters of these black holes \cite{EventHorizon,Chakhchi,Gogoi .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Using the fractional deviation from the Schwarzschild black hole shadow diameter, defined as \begin{equation}\delta = \frac{R_{sh}}{r_{sh}},\end{equation} it has been demonstrated that these constraints can be imposed on the black hole parameters through the dimensionless quantity $R_s/M$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Indeed, the $(1-\sigma)$ and $(2-\sigma)$ measurements derived from the observational data are provided in Tab.(\ref{t1 ).}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|} Black Hole Deviation (\(\delta\)) 1-\(\sigma\) Bounds 2-\(\sigma\) Bounds M87$^*$ (EHT) $-0.01^{+0.17}_{-0.17}$ $4.26 \leq \frac{R_s}{M} \leq 6.03$ $3.38 \leq \frac{R_s}{M} \leq 6.91$ Sgr A$^*$ (EHT$_{\text{VLTI}}$) $-0.08^{+0.09}_{-0.09}$ $4.31 \leq \frac{R_s}{M} \leq 5.25$ $3.85 \leq \frac{R_s}{M} \leq 5.72$ Sgr A$^*$ (EHT$_{\text{Keck}}$) $-0.04^{+0.09}_{-0.10}$ $4.47 \leq \frac{R_s}{M} \leq 5.46$ $3.95 \leq \frac{R_s}{M} \leq 5.92$ \end{tabular} \caption{Estimates and bounds for M87$^*$ and Sgr A$^*$ black holes.} \label{t1}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \begin{tabbing} \=\kill \includegraphics[scale=0.53]{ab.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.53]{ab2.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.53]{ab3.pdf} \end{tabbing} \caption{ \it Regions of cosmological rotating EMS black holes that are $(1-\sigma)$ and $(2-\sigma)$ consistent or inconsistent with the EHT data, as a function of the $\beta$ parameter. The plot shows the ratio $R_s/M$ for three values of the rotation parameter, with the charge $Q=0.2$, $\lambda=-0.1$ and the mass $M=1$.} \label{Cs1} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-078: In Fig.(\ref{Cs1 ), we illustrate the regions of cosmological rotating EMS AdS black holes that are $(1 -\sigma)$ and $(2 -\sigma)$ consistent or inconsistent with the EHT data.}
elements.sent-2411-079: These regions are shown by varying the $\beta$ parameter for three different values of the rotation parameter.
elements.sent-2411-080: Based on the figure, achieving consistency with the EHT data requires a small value of the stringy parameter $\beta$ for relevant values of the rotating parameter.
elements.sent-2411-081: However, there is a non $(1 -\sigma)$ consistency.
elements.sent-2411-082: Fixing the rotating parameter to 0.9, the shadow radii align closely with the observational $(2 - \sigma)$ constraints from EHT for M87* and Sgr A* for all the $\beta$ values less than 0.4.
elements.sent-2411-083: We move now to unveil the effect of the charge and the cosmological constant on the studied black holes.
elements.sent-2411-084: Fig.(\ref{Cs2 ) shows that cosmological rotating EMS AdS black holes $(1 -\sigma)$ and $(2 -\sigma)$ are consistent with the EHT data for very small values of the cosmological constant.}
elements.sent-2411-085: This confirms the previous study of the $\lambda$ effect on EMD black holes \cite{ .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{center} \begin{tabbing} \=\kill \includegraphics[scale=0.51]{FQ1.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.51]{FQ2.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.51]{FQ3.pdf} \end{tabbing} \caption{ \it Regions of cosmological rotating EMS black holes that are $(1-\sigma)$ and $(2-\sigma)$ consistent or inconsistent with the EHT empirical data, as a function of the charge. The plot shows the ratio $R_s/M$ for three values of the cosmological constant, with $\beta=0.4$, $a=0.5$ and $M=1$.} \label{Cs2} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-086: In this section, we approach the second optical quantity.
elements.sent-2411-087: It turns out that the essence of gravitational lensing lies in the bending of light due to gravitational fields, as predicted by Einstein general relativity, especially in the weak field limit.
elements.sent-2411-088: This phenomenon occurs when the light passes near massive objects like planets, black holes, or other matter.
elements.sent-2411-089: The weak deflection, being a consequence of this gravitational lensing, is a valuable tool in detecting non-trivial matter filaments.
elements.sent-2411-090: Understanding the weak deflection is crucial as it provides insights into the large-scale structure of the universe.
elements.sent-2411-091: To calculate the deflection angle, we will adopt the approach based on the Gauss-Bonnet theorem.
elements.sent-2411-092: The latter is one of the important method to calculate the weak deflection angle using optical geometry proposed by Gibbons and Werner \cite{A126,A127 .}
elements.sent-2411-093: This theorem establishes a connection between the intrinsic differential geometry of a surface and its topology.
elements.sent-2411-094: Considering the observer and the source at finite distance in the equatorial plane, the deflection angle can be expressed as \begin{equation}\Theta = \Psi_R - \Psi_S + \phi_{SR}\end{equation} where $\Psi_R $ and $ \Psi_S$ represent the angles between the light rays and the radial direction at the positions of the observer and the source, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-095: The angle $\Psi_{SR}$ denotes the longitudinal separation between these positions, as described in \cite{A118,A124,A117 .}
elements.sent-2411-096: To calculate these optical quantities, we can employ the algorithm developed in \cite{A126,A127,A118,A124,A117 .}
elements.sent-2411-097: Roughly, considering the impact parameter $b$ to be the ratio between the energy and the angular momentum of the light rays and taking the calculation to be in the order $\mathcal{O (M^1,\lambda^1,Q^2,a^1,\beta^1)$, we can reveal that the light deflection angle can be factorized using two contributions associated with the absence and the presence of the stringy coupling parameter $\beta$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Instead of providing very large computations, we give only the essential ones.
elements.sent-2411-099: Indeed, the deflection angle can be split into parts as follows \begin{equation}\Theta (\beta) = \Theta_{EMD}(M,a,Q,\lambda)+ \Theta_\beta(M,a,Q,\lambda) \beta\end{equation} where $\Theta_{EMD (M,a,Q,\lambda)$ is the deflection angle of the Einstein-Maxwell-dilaton black holes given by { \begin{eqnarray}\Theta_{EMD}(M,a,Q,\lambda) = \frac{4 M}{b}+\frac{16 M Q^2}{b^3} -\frac{b \lambda }{6} \left(\frac{1}{{u_R}}+\frac{1}{{u_S}}\right)-b \lambda M+\frac{8 \lambda M Q^2}{b}+\frac{b \lambda Q^2}{6 M}+\frac{2 a M}{b^2}+\frac{4 a \lambda M}{3}\notag - \frac{24 a M Q^2}{b^4} -\frac{12 a \lambda M Q^2}{b^2}\end{eqnarray} where $u_S$ and $u_R$ are the inverse of the distances from the black hole to the source and the observer, respectively.}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\Theta_{EMD}(M,a,Q,\lambda) = \frac{4 M}{b}+\frac{16 M Q^2}{b^3} -\frac{b \lambda }{6} \left(\frac{1}{{u_R}}+\frac{1}{{u_S}}\right)-b \lambda M+\frac{8 \lambda M Q^2}{b}+\frac{b \lambda Q^2}{6 M}+\frac{2 a M}{b^2}+\frac{4 a \lambda M}{3}\notag - \frac{24 a M Q^2}{b^4} -\frac{12 a \lambda M Q^2}{b^2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-100: Putting $\beta=0$, we recover the expression of the deflection angle of the light rays around EMD black holes found recently in \cite{hemd .}
elements.sent-2411-101: Moreover, the parameter $\beta$ contributes linearly to the light deviation angle.
elements.sent-2411-102: This contribution depends on the other black hole parameter.
elements.sent-2411-103: As the above discussion, the stringy parameter $\beta$ could be considered as an increasing or a decreasing parameter of the deflection angle.
elements.sent-2411-104: This is controlled by the sign of the coefficient function $\Theta_\beta(M,a,Q,\lambda)$.
elements.sent-2411-105: As the compatibility with empirical results necessitates a small value of the stringy parameter $\beta$, we consider $\beta=0.4$ to illustrate such behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-106: In Fig.(\ref{Cs5 ), we plot the deflection angle against the other parameters of the black hole to discover how the $\beta$ contribution changes their effects.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{center} \begin{tabbing} \=\kill \includegraphics[scale=0.53]{EMSQp.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.53]{EMSLp.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{A.pdf} \end{tabbing} \caption{ \it Behaviors of the deflection angles as a function of the impact parameter by varying the charge, the cosmological constant and the rotating parameters.} \label{Cs5} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-107: In Fig.(\ref{Cs5 ), we observe no difference in the behavior of the deflection angle with respect to variations in the charge and the cosmological parameter compared with the ordinary black holes.}
elements.sent-2411-108: This angle continues to decrease as a function of the cosmological constant, and the concave trend is attributed to the contribution of this cosmological parameter.
elements.sent-2411-109: Varying the charge, the deflection angle initially increases with the charge up to a critical point, beyond which the trend changes, with greater deviations occurring at smaller charge values.
elements.sent-2411-110: A notable difference is observed by varying the rotation parameter $a$.
elements.sent-2411-111: Unexpectedly, the behavior of the deflection angle against this rotation parameter differs from other ordinary cases and does not occur when we take $\beta=0$.
elements.sent-2411-112: The rotation parameter initially decreases the deflection angle for small values of the impact parameter.
elements.sent-2411-113: However, within a specific range of the impact parameter values, the rotation parameter begins to act as an increasing factor for the deflection angle, before ultimately reverting to a decreasing influence for larger impact parameter values.
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{center} \includegraphics[scale=0.55]{AAA.pdf} \caption{ \it Variation of the deflection angle in terms of the rotating parameter for different values of $\beta$ parameter.} \label{Cs15} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-114: To examine the contribution of $\beta$ more closely, in Fig.(\ref{Cs15 ), we fix the black hole parameter to be $(1 -\sigma)$ and $(2 -\sigma)$ consistent with the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) data and we plot the deflection angle as a function of the impact parameter by varying $\beta$ from 0 to 1.}
elements.sent-2411-115: The first observation from the figure is that the concavity is almost eliminated, likely due to the small value of the cosmological constant discussed earlier.
elements.sent-2411-116: We also see that the deflection angle decreases as a function of the rotation parameter, resembling the behavior of the Schwarzschild deflection angle.
elements.sent-2411-117: Moreover, the behavior of the deflection angle with respect to the $\beta$ parameter is controlled by the impact parameter values, as illustrated in the figure and supported by Eq.(\ref{bb ).}
elements.sent-2411-118: For small values of the impact parameter, the deflection angle decreases by increasing the $\beta$ parameter.
elements.sent-2411-119: For larger values of the impact parameter, however, this behavior reverses.
elements.sent-2411-120: This is expected from the metric expression for this type of black hole, where the coupling between charge and the stringy parameter $\beta$ suggests that they will contribute in a similar manner.
elements.sent-2411-121: Motivated by EHT results, we have investigated certain optical properties of four dimensional black holes with AdS geometries in EMS gravity.
elements.sent-2411-122: In particular, we have approached first the shadows of rotating black holes in EMS gravity by introducing a stringy parameter $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Using Newman-Janis method and the Hamilton-Jacobi algorithm, we have obtained various geometries, including D and cardioid ones, which depend on the rotating parameter and the stringy gravity parameter $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-124: In particular, we have shown that such a parameter can modify the previous findings.
elements.sent-2411-125: Sending this stringy parameter to zero, we have recovered the usual black hole shadows like the ones of ordinary solutions.
elements.sent-2411-126: Exploiting the EHT data, we have presented predictions for the stringy gravity parameter which could be exploited in string theory compactifications.
elements.sent-2411-127: Among others, the obtained shadow geometries have shown certain matchings with EHT collaboration data by imposing some constraints on the involved black hole parameters.
elements.sent-2411-128: Then, we have studied light behaviors near to AdS black holes.
elements.sent-2411-129: In particular, we have computed and analyzed the deflection angle of light rays near to the studied black holes.
elements.sent-2411-130: Along others, we have shown that such an optical quantity can be factorized using two contributions associated with the absence and the presence of the string coupling parameter $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-131: Enlarging the black hole moduli space, the deflection angle could be split into several stringy contributions such as $ \Theta (\beta) = \Theta_{EMD (M,a,Q,\lambda)+ \sum\limits_i \Theta_{\beta_i (M,a,Q,\lambda) \beta_i$ where $ \beta_i$ could be identified with certain stringy parameters This could open new road to explore other physical constraints on the optical quantities using string theory compactification scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Motivated by recent investigations of quantum information concepts in LHC activities, it could be interesting to implement the shadow properties of black holes in this scene.
elements.sent-2411-134: This could open new gates to understand the underlying physics.
elements.sent-2411-135: We hope to address such questions in future studies.
elements.sent-2411-136: The author would like to thank Adil Belhaj and Yassine Hassouni for collaboration, encouragement, and scientific support.
elements.sent-2411-137: She is also grateful for their valuable comments and insightful discussions.
